hook: Sea bastion of bronze cannons where Orson Welles opened his Othello
facts: Crenellated sea wall lined with old bronze cannons, several bearing European foundry marks
Designed by Theodore Cornut for Sultan Mohammed ben Abdallah in the 1760s
Orson Welles filmed scenes for his 1952 Othello on these ramparts
Wind off the Atlantic batters the parapet year-round
w: A row of bronze cannons faces the breakers along this honey-coloured sea wall, an 18th-century French-designed bastion built for a sultan who wanted a European-style port. Orson Welles opened his 1952 Othello here, Iago caged above the rocks. Salt, wind, old guns.

n: Skala du Port and Bab el Marsa
hook: Honey-stone harbour tower and 18th-century marine gate over the boats
facts: Square bastion guarding the fishing harbour, climbable for rooftop views
Bab el Marsa, the marine gate, built 1769 under Sultan Mohammed ben Abdallah
Overlooks the working sardine port and its blue boats
Carved stone gateway once opened straight onto the quays
w: Climb this stout harbour bastion for the postcard: blue boats, wheeling gulls, the medina white behind. The marine gate beside it, Bab el Marsa, went up in 1769 when a sultan turned a fishing cove into a royal port. Carved stone, sea spray, and the smell of grilling sardines.

hook: Phoenician purple-dye island, falcon reserve and abandoned prison
facts: Romans under Juba II processed murex shells here for Tyrian purple dye
Phoenician settlement on the landward side dates to the 7th century BC
Only non-Mediterranean breeding site of Eleonora's falcon
Ramsar-protected since 2005, with a closed 19th-century prison and mosque
w: Offshore lie the islands that gave Essaouira its colour: Romans crushed murex here for imperial purple, and Phoenicians camped on the far shore centuries before. Now Eleonora's falcons nest where convicts once rotted in a long-closed prison. Landing is barred; watch from the ramparts.

hook: Cobalt-blue sardine boats, gulls and quayside grills
facts: Morocco's third sardine port, with a fleet of small blue wooden boats
Boats painted blue, said locally to draw the sardines
Open-air stalls grill the morning catch beside the quays
Gulls and cats trail the boats for scraps
legend: They say the boats are painted blue because sardines are drawn to the colour
w: A working harbour, not a film set: hundreds of cobalt boats, fishermen mending nets, gulls diving for the catch. They say the hulls go blue because sardines favour the colour. Women scoop dropped fish from the cobbles to sell on. Diesel, brine, and a thousand wheeling birds.

hook: Restored synagogue turned house of Judeo-Moroccan memory
facts: Built around the Simon Attias synagogue on Rue Ziry Ibn Atiyah
Reopened 2020 as a museum and the Zafrani research centre
Bayt Dakira means 'house of memory' in Arabic
Founded under royal patronage by adviser Andre Azoulay, an Essaouira native
w: Behind a quiet mellah door, a restored synagogue keeps the memory of a town once 40 percent Jewish. 'Bayt Dakira', house of memory: scrolls, photographs, a research centre studying centuries of Jews and Muslims living wall to wall. Restored under a native son's care. Quiet, and heavy with it.

hook: Community synagogue brought back from ruin in the old mellah
facts: Built from 1850, inaugurated 1859 with funds raised by the community
Served as the mellah's communal house of worship
Rehabilitated from 2010 as part of the mellah restoration
Hosts intimate concerts during the Gnaoua festival
w: Raised by community subscription in the 1850s, this communal synagogue went dark when the mellah emptied, then crumbled for decades before careful hands brought it back from 2010. Painted bimah, worn benches, a quorum that rarely comes now. During the Gnaoua festival, music fills it again.

hook: Seaside saint's tomb drawing thousands of pilgrims each autumn
facts: Rabbi Haim Pinto lived 1743-1845 and is venerated as a tzaddik
A mausoleum was raised over his grave in 1998
Sits in the old marine cemetery, divided from the ocean by one wall
Annual hiloula pilgrimage each September draws crowds from abroad
legend: Pilgrims say prayers and petitions left at the tomb are answered
w: One wall divides this cemetery from the Atlantic; behind it, ranks of pale stones from the 1770s. In the middle sits the white tomb of Rabbi Haim Pinto, the town's saint. Each September pilgrims fly in for his hiloula, lighting candles and leaving petitions. They say the prayers get answered.

hook: Danish-founded gallery that launched Essaouira's outsider art
facts: Opened 1988 by Danish collector Frederic Damgaard, the town's first gallery
Championed untrained local fishermen and farmers as painters
Helped build Essaouira's international 'naive' and outsider art reputation
Works steeped in Gnaoua imagery and trance symbolism
w: A Dane named Damgaard wandered in, fell for the dreamlike daubs of local fishermen and farmers, and in 1988 opened the town's first gallery to sell them. Out of it grew one of Morocco's strangest art scenes: untrained painters, Gnaoua spirits, trance made visible. Outsider art with a sea breeze.

hook: Sand-swallowed fort tangled in a Jimi Hendrix legend
facts: Ruined bastion and aqueduct built under Sultan Mohammed ben Abdallah, 18th century
Stands south of the Oued Ksob mouth, half-buried by drifting sand
Locals dub it 'the Portuguese castle', though it postdates Portuguese rule
Hendrix did visit Essaouira in 1969
legend: Legend says the ruin inspired Hendrix's 'Castles Made of Sand', though the 1967 song predates his 1969 visit
w: Half-drowned in dune sand south of the river mouth, this gap-toothed sultan's fort gets miscalled Portuguese and tied to a tall tale: that it inspired Hendrix's 'Castles Made of Sand'. Lovely story, wrong order. He came in 1969; the song dropped in 1967. Wind, ruin, and a good legend.

hook: Saint who may have named the city, beside a 1916 lighthouse
facts: Sidi Mogdoul, an 11th-century Regraga saint, lies in the domed marabout
His name is thought to have mutated into 'Mogador', the city's old name
The square lighthouse was built 1914 and lit in 1916
Green-domed shrine sits hemmed in by spreading suburbs
legend: Some tales make Mogdoul a shipwrecked Scottish mariner named MacDougall, rather than a Berber saint
w: On the southern edge, a green-domed marabout holds Sidi Mogdoul, an 11th-century saint whose name, worn down over centuries, may well have become 'Mogador'. A squat 1916 lighthouse keeps watch beside him. One stubborn legend recasts him as a wrecked Scotsman, MacDougall. Take your pick.

hook: Marquetry cubbyholes in old powder magazines under the ramparts
facts: Workshops occupy vaulted rooms beneath the Skala that once stored ammunition
Specialise in marquetry from fragrant thuya burl off the Souss plain
Inlay uses lemonwood, cedar, ebony and mother-of-pearl
Trade concentrated in Essaouira since the early 19th century
w: Under the sea-rampart, the vaults that once held gunpowder now reek of fragrant thuya. Generations of marquetry men inlay burl wood with lemon, cedar and shell in cubbyholes barely wider than a doorway. A craft cornered by this town since the 1800s. Sawdust, sweet resin, patient hands.

hook: Spiritual home of Gnaoua trance music in the medina
facts: Named for Bilal, the first muezzin, revered in Gnaoua tradition
Held to be Essaouira's spiritual birthplace of Gnaoua brotherhood music
Hosts all-night lila ceremonies of trance and possession
Gnaoua music joined UNESCO's intangible heritage list in 2019
legend: The lila ceremony is held to summon and appease spirits through colour, incense and rhythm
w: Down a medina lane sits the spiritual hearth of Gnaoua, named for Bilal, Islam's first muezzin. Descendants of enslaved West Africans keep the rite here: all-night lila ceremonies of iron castanets, incense and trance. They say the music calls and calms the spirits. UNESCO-listed since 2019.
